blurb: Airy, daisy-like blooms on ferny stems — an effortless cottage-garden annual.
seasons: Pink/white/magenta blooms midsummer–frost; dies at first hard frost
wildlife: Bees, butterflies & hoverflies; finches eat the seed
habitat: Disturbed ground, roadsides, and dry open slopes

care.hardiness: Warm-season annual — killed by hard frost, so treated as an annual everywhere on the Front Range. No winter hardiness needed; direct sow or transplant after danger of frost (mid-May around Boulder).[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun is essential — at least 6–8 hours of direct light daily. In too much shade plants stretch, stems flop, and flowering drops sharply.[1][2]
care.soil: Thrives in average to poor, well-drained soil. Rich, heavily fertilized or amended ground produces lush leafy plants with few flowers and weak stems that flop in wind. Colorado's clay-loam soils are fine as long as drainage is decent; sandy or gravelly ground is ideal. Slightly acidic to neutral pH (6.0–7.5).[1][2]
care.water: Low to moderate once established — plants bloom best in lean, drier conditions. Overwatering or excessively moist soil suppresses flowering and promotes rank growth. Water deeply once a week in summer heat; less if rains are regular. Once self-established in a drift they are largely self-sufficient under Boulder's ~15–18 in annual precipitation.[1]
care.spacing: Space transplants or thin seedlings to 12–18 in apart; plants reach 2–4 ft tall and 1–2 ft wide. Planting a little closer (10–12 in) lets plants support one another against Front Range wind — useful on exposed sites.[1][2]
care.sow: Direct sow after the last frost when soil has warmed to at least 65°F — around late May in Boulder. Rake seeds into the top inch of loose soil, firm lightly, and water in. Germination takes 7–14 days at 70–75°F. Transplants started indoors 3–4 weeks before last frost are also fine, but direct sowing into a warm bed is easier and just as reliable.[1]
care.depth: Sow or cover seeds to about 1/8 in deep — cosmos needs light to germinate so barely cover and firm down. Thin to final spacing once seedlings reach 2–3 in.[1]
care.bloom: Mid-July through the first hard frost on the Front Range — the later, shorter days of high summer are what trigger heavy bloom on this day-length-sensitive plant. Deadhead faithfully in early summer to keep plants tidy; let seed set from late August onward for self-sowing.[1][2]
care.feeding: Do not fertilize. Even a single application of balanced fertilizer on average or decent soil produces tall, floppy, leaf-heavy plants that bloom poorly. On genuinely impoverished soils a single low-nitrogen dose at planting is the maximum.[1]
care.maintenance: Stake the tallest varieties (over 3 ft) in exposed spots, or plant in a drift so neighbors provide mutual support. Deadhead spent blooms through midsummer to prolong the flush; stop deadheading from late August so seed heads can ripen for self-sowing and finch feeding. Cut back to 12–18 in mid-season if plants become leggy — they regrow and rebloom.[1]
care.selfsow: A reliable and generous self-sower — allow some seed heads to ripen and shatter and cosmos will return each year in loose drifts. Seedlings are easy to recognize (ferny, thread-fine leaves) and easy to thin or transplant when small. It is not invasive and does not appear on any CO noxious weed list.[1]
care.troubles: Remarkably trouble-free. Aphids may cluster on young shoots; knock them off with water or leave for predators. Few fungal diseases in Colorado's dry air. Powdery mildew can appear in humid, crowded conditions — good spacing and lean soil prevent it. Tall varieties need wind protection or staking, not a disease issue.[1][2]
care.harvest: Allow seed heads to dry fully on the plant (they turn tan and papery) before collecting. Strip or shake seeds into a bag, dry further indoors, and store in a cool dry place through winter. Saved seed comes true to type for straight species colors.[1]
care.companions: Native grasses, zinnias, sunflowers, Mexican sunflower (Tithonia), bachelor's button, and other lean-soil annuals. The airy foliage pairs well with solid mounding plants like salvia or Russian sage in the mid-border.[1]
care.planting: Direct sow outdoors after the last frost once soil has warmed to at least 60–65°F — around late May in Boulder. Cosmos is fast-growing and direct-sown plants catch up with transplants within a week or two, so there is little advantage to starting indoors. If you do start early (3–4 weeks before last frost), use small pots and transplant carefully to minimize root disturbance. Set transplants or thin seedlings to 12–18 in apart after mid-May. In containers, use a lean, well-drained potting mix and give the pot full sun; cosmos is perfectly happy in a large container (10+ in) for the summer season. Pots dry out faster than garden beds — check moisture regularly in summer heat, though lean-and-drier is better than saturated. This is an annual killed by the first hard frost (early October on the Front Range), so container plants simply finish at frost with no overwintering needed.[1][2]
care.propagation: Seed is the only practical method — cosmos does not divide (no persistent crown or rhizome) and vegetative propagation is rarely attempted. Direct sow or start indoors as described above. For saving and sowing: allow selected seed heads to ripen and dry fully on the plant (they turn tan and narrow, like a needle), then strip and dry further indoors before storage. Saved open-pollinated seed comes true to color. The plant's most efficient 'propagation' is self-sowing: allow some seed heads to shatter in place from late August onward and cosmos will return in drifts the following year with no effort. Thin or transplant volunteers early while the taproot is still small.[1]

edible.summary: Garden cosmos is not a food plant and not an established edible — it is non-toxic to people and pets, but its petals are not a recognized culinary ingredient and the rest of the plant has no food use.[3]
edible.caution: Cosmos bipinnatus is listed as non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses and is not poisonous to people, so an accidental nibble is not a safety emergency. But it is not a documented food: while the petals of a different species (Cosmos sulphureus) are sometimes used as a colorful garnish, C. bipinnatus is not an accepted edible, so it should not be eaten as a vegetable or salad green. As with any plant in the daisy (Asteraceae) family, people with ragweed/aster allergies may react to the pollen or foliage. When in doubt, treat it as ornamental only.[3]
